evals.prompt: Perform a root cause analysis of this production incident: our checkout API started returning 500s after this afternoon's deploy. Everything we have is in api-500/incident.md.
evals.causal_chain: IMMEDIATE: unhandled TypeError in applyPaymentTimeout — setTimeout received undefined ms. MECHANISM: PAYMENT_TIMEOUT_MS = Number(process.env.PAYMENT_TIMEOUT_MS); the env var is absent in PRODUCTION, so Number(undefined) = NaN passed to setTimeout -> throws on the first checkout that hit the payment path (14:03, ~2.5min after deploy because checkout traffic is bursty — explains the trigger->failure gap). ROOT (process/systemic): config and code ship through DISCONNECTED paths — the env var was added to staging config (separate repo) but never to prod, and the PR/CI has no gate verifying required config exists in the target env; no startup validation; staging soak passed because staging HAD the var. FIVE WHYS bottoms out at: no config-completeness/schema gate linking code that reads new config to the config being present. FISHBONE: Config (drift staging vs prod), Process (PR template doesn't link config repo; review missed it), Deployment (config deploys decoupled from app deploys, no gate), Monitoring (8.5-min detection gap; threshold 10%/5min too slow for a 88% error rate), Code (no default/guard on the timeout; no test boots the real module — mocks hid it). RECURRENCE SIGNAL: the March REDIS_TLS_MODE incident is the SAME class and its preventive action ('startup validation for required env vars') was written but never scheduled — the headline finding is that postmortem actions lack follow-through.
expectations: Builds a timestamped timeline (normal -> deploy 14:00:41 -> first error 14:03:12 -> alert 14:11 -> rollback 14:26) with the detection gap called out
Immediate cause: Number(process.env.PAYMENT_TIMEOUT_MS) is NaN because the env var is missing in production, throwing in setTimeout
Root cause reaches the systemic/process level (config and code ship via disconnected paths with no validation gate), not just 'missing env var'
Explains the ~2.5 min trigger-to-failure gap via bursty checkout traffic (first payment-path request at 14:03)
Performs Five Whys that ends at a missing gate/validation, and a Fishbone touching Config, Process, Deployment, and Monitoring
Separates corrective actions (add var + safe default) from preventive actions (config schema validation / startup check in CI)
Flags the recurring-incident signal: the March incident was the same class and its action was never shipped (follow-through gap)
Uses confidence levels and does not state unverified claims as facts

evals.prompt: RCA please: our order-processing service intermittently creates duplicate orders. Details, architecture, logs and code are in duplicate-orders/incident.md.
evals.causal_chain: IMMEDIATE: a single order.create message is processed twice (consumer-2 creates order 88231, then after its pod OOM-crashes mid-processing the message is redelivered to consumer-4 which creates order 88232). MECHANISM: the consumer creates the order row and calls the warehouse, then acks LAST; when the pod is OOM-killed after the DB insert but before ack, RabbitMQ (at-least-once, quorum queue) redelivers, and the handler is NOT idempotent — a plain INSERT with auto-increment PK and no uniqueness on msg_id/paymentRef, so the second processing creates a second order. TWO HOLES LINING UP: (a) at-least-once delivery + non-idempotent consumer; (b) consumers OOM-crashing under evening peak (memory limit 256Mi) which is what triggers the redeliveries — explains the evening-peak clustering. ROOT: idempotency was never designed in — the June 13 design doc assumed 'RabbitMQ guarantees each message processed once' (false; at-least-once means at-least-once), and no reviewer challenged it. FIVE WHYS ends at: the exactly-once assumption baked into the design + no enforcement (no idempotency key, no unique constraint, no dedup). FISHBONE: Code (non-idempotent handler, ack-after-side-effects ordering), Architecture (no idempotency/dedup; DB has no constraint to fail-fast the duplicate), Infrastructure (256Mi limit causing OOM redeliveries), Process (design doc's false assumption unchallenged; no redelivery integration test), Monitoring (consumer OOM restarts un-alerted, 'self-healing'; no duplicate-order monitor — 9 dupes went unnoticed). PROPAGATION/CONTAINMENT: a unique constraint on (msg_id) or (paymentRef) would have made the duplicate INSERT fail fast = the missing last line of defense.
expectations: Reconstructs the interleaving from the logs: consumer-2 inserts 88231 then OOMs before ack -> redelivery -> consumer-4 inserts 88232
Immediate cause identified as message redelivery + non-idempotent consumer (not 'RabbitMQ is broken')
Correctly identifies the two contributing holes: at-least-once redelivery AND consumers OOM-crashing before ack (explaining evening-peak clustering)
Root cause reaches the design-level exactly-once false assumption / idempotency never designed in
Recommends idempotency as the primary fix (idempotency key or unique constraint on msg_id/paymentRef with conflict handling), not just 'retry less'
Notes the DB uniqueness constraint as a fail-fast backstop, and fixing the consumer OOM / ack ordering
Fishbone/monitoring findings include the un-alerted OOM restarts and the absent duplicate-order monitor
Uses confidence levels; keeps ruled-out or contributing factors distinct from the root cause

evals.prompt: We have a memory leak slowly crashing a long-running Node.js notifications service. Do a full root cause analysis. Everything is in memory-leak/incident.md.
evals.causal_chain: IMMEDIATE: RSS climbs with request volume until it hits the 512Mi limit and the pod is OOM-killed (2-4x/day now). MECHANISM: the renderCache Map in templates.ts (added June 2, v1.31.0) has no eviction/TTL/size bound, and its key (name:userId:locale:theme:date-for-digests) is effectively unbounded — per-user-per-day keys are near-unique (0.4% hit rate), so the Map grows ~1 entry per render forever (heap snapshot confirms: 1.9M entries, ~210MB, keys like digest:u_18422:2026-07-07). ROOT (process): an unbounded cache shipped WITHOUT REVIEW via the hotfix/* branch bypass, used for a non-emergency Friday perf complaint; and the cache doesn't even solve the reported problem (the slowness was later traced to a DB query, not rendering — the cache's hit rate is ~0, pure downside). FIVE WHYS ends at: the hotfix bypass allows unreviewed prod changes for non-emergencies (a review would likely have caught the unbounded key space), AND there is no soak/endurance test (5-min max load test can't surface a slow leak) AND no memory-trend alert (detection was accidental, via restart counts). FISHBONE: Code (unbounded cache, no eviction), Process (hotfix bypass misused, no review), Monitoring (no memory alert; OOMs 'self-healing' and unnoticed for weeks; ~40 double-sent emails from mid-render crashes), Deployment/CI (no soak test), plus the cache is ineffective (wrong fix for a misdiagnosed problem). CORRECTIVE vs PREVENTIVE: corrective = bound/evict the cache or remove it (it gives ~no benefit); preventive = restrict hotfix bypass, add memory-trend alerting, add a soak test, fix the double-send ledger ordering.
expectations: Builds the degradation timeline (May stable -> June 2 v1.31.0 -> first OOM June 9 -> now 2-4/day) and ties onset to the specific deploy
Immediate/mechanism: the unbounded renderCache Map with a near-unique key growing without eviction until OOM (cites the heap snapshot evidence)
Identifies that the cache is nearly useless (0.4% hit rate) and was a fix for a misdiagnosed problem (real cause was a DB query)
Root cause reaches process level: unreviewed hotfix-bypass merge of an unbounded cache, plus no soak test and no memory alerting
Corrective action (bound/evict/remove the cache) is separated from preventive actions (hotfix policy, memory alert, soak/endurance test in CI)
Monitoring findings include the absence of memory-trend alerting (weeks of undetected OOM) and the double-sent-email side effect
Performs Five Whys ending at actionable process/architecture gaps and a Fishbone across Code/Process/Monitoring/CI
Uses confidence levels; distinguishes verified facts (heap snapshot, hit rate) from inferences

evals.prompt: Our support AI agent keeps confidently giving customers wrong order statuses (says shipped when actually cancelled). Perform a root cause analysis. Details, transcript, server logs and code are in agent-hallucination/incident.md.
evals.causal_chain: IMMEDIATE: the agent tells customers the order shipped when the get_order_status tool returned an EMPTY STRING, not a real status — the model, given a warm/reassuring instruction and an empty tool result with no 'unknown' handling, fabricated a plausible positive answer. This is NOT primarily a model hallucination — it's the model filling a gap created by a SILENT TOOL FAILURE. MECHANISM: Monday's PR #77 changed the orders-mcp handler's catch block from `throw e` to `return ""` ('so the agent doesn't crash on DB blips'); under peak concurrency the connection pool (size 5) is exhausted by up to 20 concurrent sessions, the query times out, the handler swallows the error and returns "", and the agent platform logs only the final tool_result string (no separate tool-error telemetry), so the failure is invisible. Explains the peak-correlation (pool exhaustion is load-dependent) and intermittence. ROOT: errors are coerced to empty successful-looking results instead of surfaced (swallowed-error anti-pattern at the tool boundary), the agent has no contract for empty/unknown tool output and no output validation, and there is no tool-call telemetry distinguishing failure from result. FIVE WHYS ends at: the tool boundary converts failures into indistinguishable-from-success empty values + no observability to catch it + no eval/regression harness for tool-failure behavior. FISHBONE: Code (swallowed error returning ''), Architecture (pool size 5 vs 20 concurrent; no backpressure), Dependencies/Config (pool sizing), Monitoring (no tool-error telemetry — can't tell model failure from tool failure), Process (QA tested only happy path with warm pool; no agent evals for tool failures), AI-specific (no 'if tool returns unknown, say you don't know' instruction, no output validation). CORRECTIVE: restore throwing / return an explicit error status the agent must handle; raise pool size / add backpressure. PREVENTIVE: never coerce tool errors to empty; validate tool outputs; add tool-call error telemetry; add agent evals for tool-failure paths; instruct the agent to refuse to answer on unknown.
expectations: Correctly reframes the 'hallucination' as the model filling a gap left by a SILENT TOOL FAILURE, not primarily a model defect
Traces the mechanism: PR #77 changed throw to return empty; pool exhaustion (size 5 vs 20 concurrent) at peak causes timeouts; empty string returned and logged as a normal result
Explains the peak/intermittent correlation via load-dependent connection-pool exhaustion
Root cause reaches the tool-boundary error-swallowing anti-pattern PLUS missing tool-error telemetry PLUS no agent handling/validation of empty output
Builds a timeline tying onset to Monday's orders-mcp deploy and correlating failures with peak concurrency
Corrective (surface the error / fix pool sizing) separated from preventive (no error coercion, output validation, tool-call telemetry, agent evals, unknown-handling instruction)
Monitoring finding centers on the inability to distinguish tool failure from tool result (no tool-error telemetry)
Uses confidence levels and evidence (transcript + server log) rather than blaming the model
